Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Okunitama Shrine |画像= [[File:大國玉神社 拝殿.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall |所在地 = {{jalink|Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273}}{{jalink|Sakuragawa|Q990350}} Okunitama 1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|Q276944|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Shikinaisha}} (minor)
former {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system#various shrines|Q712534|Gōsha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |別名 = |例祭 = {{jalink|November 15|Q2982}} |神事 = Kuwe no Matsuri (Kuwauchi Festival)
Sayado Meguri Festival |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki|label=Okunitama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:大國玉神社 拝殿の扁額.jpg|thumb|right|245px|Plaque on the worship hall]] [[File:大國玉神社 本殿.jpg|thumb|right|245px|Main hall]] '''Okunitama Shrine''' (おおくにたまじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Sakuragawa|Q990350}}, {{jalink|Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273}}, enshrining {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}. Its nickname is Myōjin-samaIbaraki Prefectural Shrine Association: [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/ibaraki/kensei/jinja/12178.html Shrine guide within the prefecture]. Retrieved November 16, 2013.. Its former name was Kashima Daimyōjin (Kashima Myōjin)''Shinpen Hitachi no Kuni-shi''.. It is listed as a {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Hitachi Province|Q1055353}}, Makabe District, minor shrine). Its former rank was {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system#various shrines|Q712534|Gōsha}}. It is also written in old characters as '''大國玉神社'''. === History === The founding date is unknown. Shrine tradition says it was founded in the {{jalink|Yōrō|Q1373061}} era ({{jalink|717|Q23532|717}}–{{jalink|724|Q23549}}). Another tradition places it in the {{jalink|Tenchō|Q1373138}} era ({{jalink|824|Q30353|824}}–{{jalink|834|Q30381}}). It is an ancient shrine recorded in the {{jalink|Rikkokushi|Q1151168}} and the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}. * {{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}} ** Vol. 6: In the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Ninmyō|Q349271}}, 3rd month of {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 4 ({{jalink|837|Q25093}}), together with Sashino no Kami of Niihari District, it appears as "'''Ōkunitama no Kami of Makabe District'''" with the note "並預官社。以比年特有靈驗也" (it was admitted to the government shrine register due to especially notable divine efficacy in that year). ** Vol. 15: In the same era, year 12 ({{jalink|846|Q30454}}), "奉授常陸國无位'''大國玉神'''從五位下" (granted Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade). * {{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}} ** Vol. 5: In the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Seiwa|Q349243}}, 9th month of {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 3 ({{jalink|861|Q23913}}), "授常陸國從五位下'''主玉神'''從五位上" (promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade). ** Among historical records, only the ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' records the deity name as "Shutama no Kami"; whether this is the same deity as Ōkunitama no Kami is unclear. Since there are three shrines in Ibaraki claiming to correspond to "Shutama no Kami" (Oishi Shrine in Hokota City, and Kamo-Ōkami-Miko-Shutama Shrine in Sakuragawa City), this is treated as a {{jalink|ronsha|Q135022904}} (disputed corresponding shrine). * {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} ** In {{jalink|Engi|Q1374325}} 5 (927), listed as one minor shrine in Makabe District, Hitachi Province: "'''Ōkunitama Shrine'''" (one of {{jalink|Hitachi Province|Q1055353}}'s 28 shrines). In the 11th month of {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}} 12 ({{jalink|1699|Q7710}}), {{jalink|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|Q1194944}} dedicated banners of the four gods and halberd-banners of the sun and moon. In the 4th month of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 5 ({{jalink|1872|Q7753}}), it was ranked as a gōsha. In October {{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} 4 ({{jalink|1992|Q2060}}), shrine buildings were renovated. Old regional gazetteers introduce a scenic site called the "Seven Wells of Okunitama": Miyamae no I, Kugutsu I, Kōshinmae I, Kisaki no I, Fukusen Koshiki I, Fukusen Kome I, and Fukusen Sake I. File:大國玉神社 生鳥居１.jpg|Eastern ikitorii (living torii) File:大國玉神社 生鳥居２.jpg|Western ikitorii (living torii) === Enshrined deities === ; Main deity : {{jalink|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|Q276944|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} ; Associated deities : {{jalink|Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Kamo Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto|Q11634943|Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto}} ''Shinpen Hitachi no Kuni-shi'' states: "Called Kashima Daimyōjin. According to shrine tradition, there were two enshrined deities: the east was called Nantai-gū, and the west Nyotai-gū, also called Monoimi-gū." In earlier times it was called Kashima Daimyōjin, and there was a paired two-shrine structure of a male-deity shrine and female-deity shrine. The integration of Nyotai-gū is described as "at the beginning of Meiji, the priestly office of the western shrine was dissolved and succeeded by the priest of the eastern shrine"Hitachi no Kuni-kei Sugiyama Shiki., indicating that this occurred in the Meiji period. The present shrine site is considered the continuation of Nantai-gū. The old site of Nyotai-gū is unknown, but Kisaki Shrine in the precincts enshrines Ōkuninushi's principal consort {{jalink|Suseribime|Q8191715|Suseribime-no-Mikoto}}. There is also another Kisaki Shrine east of Ōkunitama Shrine (an independent shrine: {{jalink|tanritsu|Q7882536|independent shrine}}), but its deity is "Kimi no Gozen," wife of {{jalink|Taira no Masakado|Q1207652}}, and its {{jalink|shintai}} is a wooden female figure (Kisaki Shrine sacred image; designated cultural property of Sakuragawa City). Regarding the deity, one account says: "Perhaps Kashima Ōkami was enshrined; this is also evidenced by 23 songs transmitted at this shrine, all praising the divine power of Kashima deity." It further argues that "Ōkunitama-no-Kami" or "Kuni-gami" was an honorific title for deities who pacified each province, and in Hitachi Province it was another name for Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto. === Precinct shrines === * Kisaki Shrine ({{jalink|Suseribime|Q8191715|Suseribime-no-Mikoto}}) * Yasaka Shrine ({{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}) * Aoma Shrine ({{jalink|Amaterasu|Q455602|Amaterasu-Ōmikami}}, {{jalink|Tsukuyomi|Q595520|Tsukuyomi-no-Kami}}, {{jalink|Ame-no-Minakanushi|Q2842779}}) * Shichijūgohashira Shrine (collective shrine of the myriad deities; Yaoyorozu no Kami) File:大國玉神社 摂社 青麻神社.jpg|Aoma Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 七拾五柱神社（八百萬神集合社）.jpg|Shichijūgohashira Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 后神社.jpg|Kisaki Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 八坂神社.jpg|Yasaka Shrine === Festivals === * {{jalink|January 3|Q2156}}: Kuwe no Matsuri (Kuwauchi Festival) – a {{jalink|ta-asobi|Q11577415}} ritual praying for a good harvest; designated intangible folk cultural property of Sakuragawa City. * {{jalink|January 4|Q2168}}: Sayado Meguri Festival – formally called "Ōkunitama Shrine Mass Procession of the Seventy-Five Subsidiary Shrines"; participants carry a large sacred sakaki branch and beat drums while touring subsidiary shrines in the Okunitama district to pray for household safety; designated intangible folk cultural property of Sakuragawa City. * {{jalink|November 15|Q2982}}: Annual festival === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} {{茨城県の神社一覧目次}} {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおくにたましんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Okunitama Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Okunitama Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:茨城県の神社]] [[Category:常陸国の式内社]] [[Category:茨城県の旧郷社]] [[Category:桜川市の建築物]] [[Category:桜川市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Okunitama Shrine |画像= [[File:大國玉神社 拝殿.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall |所在地 = {{jalink|Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273}}{{jalink|Sakuragawa|Q990350}} Okunitama 1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|Q276944|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Shikinaisha}} (minor)
former {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system#various shrines|Q712534|Gōsha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |別名 = |例祭 = {{jalink|November 15|Q2982}} |神事 = Kuwe no Matsuri (Kuwauchi Festival)
Sayado Meguri Festival |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki|label=Okunitama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:大國玉神社 拝殿の扁額.jpg|thumb|right|245px|Plaque on the worship hall]] [[File:大國玉神社 本殿.jpg|thumb|right|245px|Main hall]] '''Okunitama Shrine''' (おおくにたまじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Sakuragawa|Q990350}}, {{jalink|Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273}}, enshrining {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}. Its nickname is Myōjin-samaIbaraki Prefectural Shrine Association: [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/ibaraki/kensei/jinja/12178.html Shrine guide within the prefecture]. Retrieved November 16, 2013.. Its former name was Kashima Daimyōjin (Kashima Myōjin)''Shinpen Hitachi no Kuni-shi''.. It is listed as a {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Hitachi Province|Q1055353}}, Makabe District, minor shrine). Its former rank was {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system#various shrines|Q712534|Gōsha}}. It is also written in old characters as '''大國玉神社'''. === History === The founding date is unknown. Shrine tradition says it was founded in the {{jalink|Yōrō|Q1373061}} era ({{jalink|717|Q23532|717}}–{{jalink|724|Q23549}}). Another tradition places it in the {{jalink|Tenchō|Q1373138}} era ({{jalink|824|Q30353|824}}–{{jalink|834|Q30381}}). It is an ancient shrine recorded in the {{jalink|Rikkokushi|Q1151168}} and the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}. * {{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}} ** Vol. 6: In the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Ninmyō|Q349271}}, 3rd month of {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 4 ({{jalink|837|Q25093}}), together with Sashino no Kami of Niihari District, it appears as "'''Ōkunitama no Kami of Makabe District'''" with the note "並預官社。以比年特有靈驗也" (it was admitted to the government shrine register due to especially notable divine efficacy in that year). ** Vol. 15: In the same era, year 12 ({{jalink|846|Q30454}}), "奉授常陸國无位'''大國玉神'''從五位下" (granted Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade). * {{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}} ** Vol. 5: In the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Seiwa|Q349243}}, 9th month of {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 3 ({{jalink|861|Q23913}}), "授常陸國從五位下'''主玉神'''從五位上" (promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade). ** Among historical records, only the ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' records the deity name as "Shutama no Kami"; whether this is the same deity as Ōkunitama no Kami is unclear. Since there are three shrines in Ibaraki claiming to correspond to "Shutama no Kami" (Oishi Shrine in Hokota City, and Kamo-Ōkami-Miko-Shutama Shrine in Sakuragawa City), this is treated as a {{jalink|ronsha|Q135022904}} (disputed corresponding shrine). * {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} ** In {{jalink|Engi|Q1374325}} 5 (927), listed as one minor shrine in Makabe District, Hitachi Province: "'''Ōkunitama Shrine'''" (one of {{jalink|Hitachi Province|Q1055353}}'s 28 shrines). In the 11th month of {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}} 12 ({{jalink|1699|Q7710}}), {{jalink|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|Q1194944}} dedicated banners of the four gods and halberd-banners of the sun and moon. In the 4th month of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 5 ({{jalink|1872|Q7753}}), it was ranked as a gōsha. In October {{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} 4 ({{jalink|1992|Q2060}}), shrine buildings were renovated. Old regional gazetteers introduce a scenic site called the "Seven Wells of Okunitama": Miyamae no I, Kugutsu I, Kōshinmae I, Kisaki no I, Fukusen Koshiki I, Fukusen Kome I, and Fukusen Sake I. File:大國玉神社 生鳥居１.jpg|Eastern ikitorii (living torii) File:大國玉神社 生鳥居２.jpg|Western ikitorii (living torii) === Enshrined deities === ; Main deity : {{jalink|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|Q276944|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} ; Associated deities : {{jalink|Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Kamo Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto|Q11634943|Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto}} ''Shinpen Hitachi no Kuni-shi'' states: "Called Kashima Daimyōjin. According to shrine tradition, there were two enshrined deities: the east was called Nantai-gū, and the west Nyotai-gū, also called Monoimi-gū." In earlier times it was called Kashima Daimyōjin, and there was a paired two-shrine structure of a male-deity shrine and female-deity shrine. The integration of Nyotai-gū is described as "at the beginning of Meiji, the priestly office of the western shrine was dissolved and succeeded by the priest of the eastern shrine"Hitachi no Kuni-kei Sugiyama Shiki., indicating that this occurred in the Meiji period. The present shrine site is considered the continuation of Nantai-gū. The old site of Nyotai-gū is unknown, but Kisaki Shrine in the precincts enshrines Ōkuninushi's principal consort {{jalink|Suseribime|Q8191715|Suseribime-no-Mikoto}}. There is also another Kisaki Shrine east of Ōkunitama Shrine (an independent shrine: {{jalink|tanritsu|Q7882536|independent shrine}}), but its deity is "Kimi no Gozen," wife of {{jalink|Taira no Masakado|Q1207652}}, and its {{jalink|shintai}} is a wooden female figure (Kisaki Shrine sacred image; designated cultural property of Sakuragawa City). Regarding the deity, one account says: "Perhaps Kashima Ōkami was enshrined; this is also evidenced by 23 songs transmitted at this shrine, all praising the divine power of Kashima deity." It further argues that "Ōkunitama-no-Kami" or "Kuni-gami" was an honorific title for deities who pacified each province, and in Hitachi Province it was another name for Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto. === Precinct shrines === * Kisaki Shrine ({{jalink|Suseribime|Q8191715|Suseribime-no-Mikoto}}) * Yasaka Shrine ({{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}) * Aoma Shrine ({{jalink|Amaterasu|Q455602|Amaterasu-Ōmikami}}, {{jalink|Tsukuyomi|Q595520|Tsukuyomi-no-Kami}}, {{jalink|Ame-no-Minakanushi|Q2842779}}) * Shichijūgohashira Shrine (collective shrine of the myriad deities; Yaoyorozu no Kami) File:大國玉神社 摂社 青麻神社.jpg|Aoma Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 七拾五柱神社（八百萬神集合社）.jpg|Shichijūgohashira Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 后神社.jpg|Kisaki Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 八坂神社.jpg|Yasaka Shrine === Festivals === * {{jalink|January 3|Q2156}}: Kuwe no Matsuri (Kuwauchi Festival) – a {{jalink|ta-asobi|Q11577415}} ritual praying for a good harvest; designated intangible folk cultural property of Sakuragawa City. * {{jalink|January 4|Q2168}}: Sayado Meguri Festival – formally called "Ōkunitama Shrine Mass Procession of the Seventy-Five Subsidiary Shrines"; participants carry a large sacred sakaki branch and beat drums while touring subsidiary shrines in the Okunitama district to pray for household safety; designated intangible folk cultural property of Sakuragawa City. * {{jalink|November 15|Q2982}}: Annual festival === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} {{茨城県の神社一覧目次}} {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおくにたましんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Okunitama Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Okunitama Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:茨城県の神社]] [[Category:常陸国の式内社]] [[Category:茨城県の旧郷社]] [[Category:桜川市の建築物]] [[Category:桜川市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

(Sakuragawa) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '茨城県桜川市大国玉1', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Okunitama-jinja (Sakuragawa)}} [[JA:大国玉神社 (桜川市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11433665}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Okunitama Shrine |画像= [[File:大國玉神社 拝殿.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273}}{{ill|lt=Sakuragawa|Q990350|WD=Q990350}} Okunitama 1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor)
former {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system#various shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Gōsha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |別名 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982}} |神事 = Kuwe no Matsuri (Kuwauchi Festival)
Sayado Meguri Festival |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki|label=Okunitama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:大國玉神社 拝殿の扁額.jpg|thumb|right|245px|Plaque on the worship hall]] [[File:大國玉神社 本殿.jpg|thumb|right|245px|Main hall]] '''Okunitama Shrine''' (おおくにたまじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Sakuragawa|Q990350|WD=Q990350}}, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273}}, enshrining {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}. Its nickname is Myōjin-samaIbaraki Prefectural Shrine Association: [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/ibaraki/kensei/jinja/12178.html Shrine guide within the prefecture]. Retrieved November 16, 2013.. Its former name was Kashima Daimyōjin (Kashima Myōjin)''Shinpen Hitachi no Kuni-shi''.. It is listed as a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|lt=Hitachi Province|Q1055353|WD=Q1055353}}, Makabe District, minor shrine). Its former rank was {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system#various shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Gōsha}}. It is also written in old characters as '''大國玉神社'''. === History === The founding date is unknown. Shrine tradition says it was founded in the {{ill|lt=Yōrō|Q1373061|WD=Q1373061}} era ({{ill|lt=717|Q23532|WD=Q23532|lt=717}}–{{ill|lt=724|Q23549|WD=Q23549}}). Another tradition places it in the {{ill|lt=Tenchō|Q1373138|WD=Q1373138}} era ({{ill|lt=824|Q30353|WD=Q30353|lt=824}}–{{ill|lt=834|Q30381|WD=Q30381}}). It is an ancient shrine recorded in the {{ill|lt=Rikkokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168}} and the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}. * {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301}} ** Vol. 6: In the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Ninmyō|Q349271|WD=Q349271}}, 3rd month of {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 4 ({{ill|lt=837|Q25093|WD=Q25093}}), together with Sashino no Kami of Niihari District, it appears as "'''Ōkunitama no Kami of Makabe District'''" with the note "並預官社。以比年特有靈驗也" (it was admitted to the government shrine register due to especially notable divine efficacy in that year). ** Vol. 15: In the same era, year 12 ({{ill|lt=846|Q30454|WD=Q30454}}), "奉授常陸國无位'''大國玉神'''從五位下" (granted Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade). * {{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}} ** Vol. 5: In the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243}}, 9th month of {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 3 ({{ill|lt=861|Q23913|WD=Q23913}}), "授常陸國從五位下'''主玉神'''從五位上" (promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade). ** Among historical records, only the ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' records the deity name as "Shutama no Kami"; whether this is the same deity as Ōkunitama no Kami is unclear. Since there are three shrines in Ibaraki claiming to correspond to "Shutama no Kami" (Oishi Shrine in Hokota City, and Kamo-Ōkami-Miko-Shutama Shrine in Sakuragawa City), this is treated as a {{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}} (disputed corresponding shrine). * {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} ** In {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325}} 5 (927), listed as one minor shrine in Makabe District, Hitachi Province: "'''Ōkunitama Shrine'''" (one of {{ill|lt=Hitachi Province|Q1055353|WD=Q1055353}}'s 28 shrines). In the 11th month of {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1699|Q7710|WD=Q7710}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsukuni|Q1194944|WD=Q1194944}} dedicated banners of the four gods and halberd-banners of the sun and moon. In the 4th month of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}), it was ranked as a gōsha. In October {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1992|Q2060|WD=Q2060}}), shrine buildings were renovated. Old regional gazetteers introduce a scenic site called the "Seven Wells of Okunitama": Miyamae no I, Kugutsu I, Kōshinmae I, Kisaki no I, Fukusen Koshiki I, Fukusen Kome I, and Fukusen Sake I. File:大國玉神社 生鳥居１.jpg|Eastern ikitorii (living torii) File:大國玉神社 生鳥居２.jpg|Western ikitorii (living torii) === Enshrined deities === ; Main deity : {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} ; Associated deities : {{ill|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kamo Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto|Q11634943|WD=Q11634943|lt=Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto}} ''Shinpen Hitachi no Kuni-shi'' states: "Called Kashima Daimyōjin. According to shrine tradition, there were two enshrined deities: the east was called Nantai-gū, and the west Nyotai-gū, also called Monoimi-gū." In earlier times it was called Kashima Daimyōjin, and there was a paired two-shrine structure of a male-deity shrine and female-deity shrine. The integration of Nyotai-gū is described as "at the beginning of Meiji, the priestly office of the western shrine was dissolved and succeeded by the priest of the eastern shrine"Hitachi no Kuni-kei Sugiyama Shiki., indicating that this occurred in the Meiji period. The present shrine site is considered the continuation of Nantai-gū. The old site of Nyotai-gū is unknown, but Kisaki Shrine in the precincts enshrines Ōkuninushi's principal consort {{ill|lt=Suseribime|Q8191715|WD=Q8191715|lt=Suseribime-no-Mikoto}}. There is also another Kisaki Shrine east of Ōkunitama Shrine (an independent shrine: {{ill|lt=tanritsu|Q7882536|WD=Q7882536|lt=independent shrine}}), but its deity is "Kimi no Gozen," wife of {{ill|lt=Taira no Masakado|Q1207652|WD=Q1207652}}, and its {{ill||lt=shintai|UNKNOWN}} is a wooden female figure (Kisaki Shrine sacred image; designated cultural property of Sakuragawa City). Regarding the deity, one account says: "Perhaps Kashima Ōkami was enshrined; this is also evidenced by 23 songs transmitted at this shrine, all praising the divine power of Kashima deity." It further argues that "Ōkunitama-no-Kami" or "Kuni-gami" was an honorific title for deities who pacified each province, and in Hitachi Province it was another name for Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto. === Precinct shrines === * Kisaki Shrine ({{ill|lt=Suseribime|Q8191715|WD=Q8191715|lt=Suseribime-no-Mikoto}}) * Yasaka Shrine ({{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}) * Aoma Shrine ({{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami}}, {{ill|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Minakanushi|Q2842779|WD=Q2842779}}) * Shichijūgohashira Shrine (collective shrine of the myriad deities; Yaoyorozu no Kami) File:大國玉神社 摂社 青麻神社.jpg|Aoma Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 七拾五柱神社（八百萬神集合社）.jpg|Shichijūgohashira Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 后神社.jpg|Kisaki Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 八坂神社.jpg|Yasaka Shrine === Festivals === * {{ill|lt=January 3|Q2156|WD=Q2156}}: Kuwe no Matsuri (Kuwauchi Festival) – a {{ill|lt=ta-asobi|Q11577415|WD=Q11577415}} ritual praying for a good harvest; designated intangible folk cultural property of Sakuragawa City. * {{ill|lt=January 4|Q2168|WD=Q2168}}: Sayado Meguri Festival – formally called "Ōkunitama Shrine Mass Procession of the Seventy-Five Subsidiary Shrines"; participants carry a large sacred sakaki branch and beat drums while touring subsidiary shrines in the Okunitama district to pray for household safety; designated intangible folk cultural property of Sakuragawa City. * {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982}}: Annual festival === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} {{茨城県の神社一覧目次}} {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおくにたましんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Okunitama Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Okunitama Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:茨城県の神社]] [[Category:常陸国の式内社]] [[Category:茨城県の旧郷社]] [[Category:桜川市の建築物]] [[Category:桜川市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

(Sakuragawa) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '茨城県桜川市大国玉1', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Okunitama-jinja (Sakuragawa)}} [[JA:大国玉神社 (桜川市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11433665}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Okunitama Shrine |画像= [[File:大國玉神社 拝殿.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273}}{{ill|lt=Sakuragawa|Q990350|WD=Q990350}} Okunitama 1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor)
former {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system#various shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Gōsha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |別名 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982}} |神事 = Kuwe no Matsuri (Kuwauchi Festival)
Sayado Meguri Festival |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki|label=Okunitama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:大國玉神社 拝殿の扁額.jpg|thumb|right|245px|Plaque on the worship hall]] [[File:大國玉神社 本殿.jpg|thumb|right|245px|Main hall]] '''Okunitama Shrine''' (おおくにたまじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Sakuragawa|Q990350|WD=Q990350}}, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273}}, enshrining {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}. Its nickname is Myōjin-samaIbaraki Prefectural Shrine Association: [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/ibaraki/kensei/jinja/12178.html Shrine guide within the prefecture]. Retrieved November 16, 2013.. Its former name was Kashima Daimyōjin (Kashima Myōjin)''Shinpen Hitachi no Kuni-shi''.. It is listed as a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|lt=Hitachi Province|Q1055353|WD=Q1055353}}, Makabe District, minor shrine). Its former rank was {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system#various shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Gōsha}}. It is also written in old characters as '''大國玉神社'''. === History === The founding date is unknown. Shrine tradition says it was founded in the {{ill|lt=Yōrō|Q1373061|WD=Q1373061}} era ({{ill|lt=717|Q23532|WD=Q23532|lt=717}}–{{ill|lt=724|Q23549|WD=Q23549}}). Another tradition places it in the {{ill|lt=Tenchō|Q1373138|WD=Q1373138}} era ({{ill|lt=824|Q30353|WD=Q30353|lt=824}}–{{ill|lt=834|Q30381|WD=Q30381}}). It is an ancient shrine recorded in the {{ill|lt=Rikkokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168}} and the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}. * {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301}} ** Vol. 6: In the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Ninmyō|Q349271|WD=Q349271}}, 3rd month of {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 4 ({{ill|lt=837|Q25093|WD=Q25093}}), together with Sashino no Kami of Niihari District, it appears as "'''Ōkunitama no Kami of Makabe District'''" with the note "並預官社。以比年特有靈驗也" (it was admitted to the government shrine register due to especially notable divine efficacy in that year). ** Vol. 15: In the same era, year 12 ({{ill|lt=846|Q30454|WD=Q30454}}), "奉授常陸國无位'''大國玉神'''從五位下" (granted Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade). * {{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}} ** Vol. 5: In the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243}}, 9th month of {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 3 ({{ill|lt=861|Q23913|WD=Q23913}}), "授常陸國從五位下'''主玉神'''從五位上" (promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade). ** Among historical records, only the ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' records the deity name as "Shutama no Kami"; whether this is the same deity as Ōkunitama no Kami is unclear. Since there are three shrines in Ibaraki claiming to correspond to "Shutama no Kami" (Oishi Shrine in Hokota City, and Kamo-Ōkami-Miko-Shutama Shrine in Sakuragawa City), this is treated as a {{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}} (disputed corresponding shrine). * {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} ** In {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325}} 5 (927), listed as one minor shrine in Makabe District, Hitachi Province: "'''Ōkunitama Shrine'''" (one of {{ill|lt=Hitachi Province|Q1055353|WD=Q1055353}}'s 28 shrines). In the 11th month of {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1699|Q7710|WD=Q7710}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsukuni|Q1194944|WD=Q1194944}} dedicated banners of the four gods and halberd-banners of the sun and moon. In the 4th month of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}), it was ranked as a gōsha. In October {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1992|Q2060|WD=Q2060}}), shrine buildings were renovated. Old regional gazetteers introduce a scenic site called the "Seven Wells of Okunitama": Miyamae no I, Kugutsu I, Kōshinmae I, Kisaki no I, Fukusen Koshiki I, Fukusen Kome I, and Fukusen Sake I. File:大國玉神社 生鳥居１.jpg|Eastern ikitorii (living torii) File:大國玉神社 生鳥居２.jpg|Western ikitorii (living torii) === Enshrined deities === ; Main deity : {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} ; Associated deities : {{ill|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kamo Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto|Q11634943|WD=Q11634943|lt=Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto}} ''Shinpen Hitachi no Kuni-shi'' states: "Called Kashima Daimyōjin. According to shrine tradition, there were two enshrined deities: the east was called Nantai-gū, and the west Nyotai-gū, also called Monoimi-gū." In earlier times it was called Kashima Daimyōjin, and there was a paired two-shrine structure of a male-deity shrine and female-deity shrine. The integration of Nyotai-gū is described as "at the beginning of Meiji, the priestly office of the western shrine was dissolved and succeeded by the priest of the eastern shrine"Hitachi no Kuni-kei Sugiyama Shiki., indicating that this occurred in the Meiji period. The present shrine site is considered the continuation of Nantai-gū. The old site of Nyotai-gū is unknown, but Kisaki Shrine in the precincts enshrines Ōkuninushi's principal consort {{ill|lt=Suseribime|Q8191715|WD=Q8191715|lt=Suseribime-no-Mikoto}}. There is also another Kisaki Shrine east of Ōkunitama Shrine (an independent shrine: {{ill|lt=tanritsu|Q7882536|WD=Q7882536|lt=independent shrine}}), but its deity is "Kimi no Gozen," wife of {{ill|lt=Taira no Masakado|Q1207652|WD=Q1207652}}, and its {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shintai|UNKNOWN}} is a wooden female figure (Kisaki Shrine sacred image; designated cultural property of Sakuragawa City). Regarding the deity, one account says: "Perhaps Kashima Ōkami was enshrined; this is also evidenced by 23 songs transmitted at this shrine, all praising the divine power of Kashima deity." It further argues that "Ōkunitama-no-Kami" or "Kuni-gami" was an honorific title for deities who pacified each province, and in Hitachi Province it was another name for Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto. === Precinct shrines === * Kisaki Shrine ({{ill|lt=Suseribime|Q8191715|WD=Q8191715|lt=Suseribime-no-Mikoto}}) * Yasaka Shrine ({{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}) * Aoma Shrine ({{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami}}, {{ill|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Minakanushi|Q2842779|WD=Q2842779}}) * Shichijūgohashira Shrine (collective shrine of the myriad deities; Yaoyorozu no Kami) File:大國玉神社 摂社 青麻神社.jpg|Aoma Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 七拾五柱神社（八百萬神集合社）.jpg|Shichijūgohashira Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 后神社.jpg|Kisaki Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 八坂神社.jpg|Yasaka Shrine === Festivals === * {{ill|lt=January 3|Q2156|WD=Q2156}}: Kuwe no Matsuri (Kuwauchi Festival) – a {{ill|lt=ta-asobi|Q11577415|WD=Q11577415}} ritual praying for a good harvest; designated intangible folk cultural property of Sakuragawa City. * {{ill|lt=January 4|Q2168|WD=Q2168}}: Sayado Meguri Festival – formally called "Ōkunitama Shrine Mass Procession of the Seventy-Five Subsidiary Shrines"; participants carry a large sacred sakaki branch and beat drums while touring subsidiary shrines in the Okunitama district to pray for household safety; designated intangible folk cultural property of Sakuragawa City. * {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982}}: Annual festival === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} {{茨城県の神社一覧目次}} {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおくにたましんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Okunitama Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Okunitama Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:茨城県の神社]] [[Category:常陸国の式内社]] [[Category:茨城県の旧郷社]] [[Category:桜川市の建築物]] [[Category:桜川市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

former {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system#various shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Gōsha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |別名 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982}} |神事 = Kuwe no Matsuri (Kuwauchi Festival)
Sayado Meguri Festival |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki|label=Okunitama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:大國玉神社 拝殿の扁額.jpg|thumb|right|245px|Plaque on the worship hall]] [[File:大國玉神社 本殿.jpg|thumb|right|245px|Main hall]] '''Okunitama Shrine''' (おおくにたまじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Sakuragawa|Q990350|WD=Q990350}}, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273}}, enshrining {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}. Its nickname is Myōjin-samaIbaraki Prefectural Shrine Association: [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/ibaraki/kensei/jinja/12178.html Shrine guide within the prefecture]. Retrieved November 16, 2013.. Its former name was Kashima Daimyōjin (Kashima Myōjin)''Shinpen Hitachi no Kuni-shi''.. It is listed as a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|lt=Hitachi Province|Q1055353|WD=Q1055353}}, Makabe District, minor shrine). Its former rank was {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system#various shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Gōsha}}. It is also written in old characters as '''大國玉神社'''. === History === The founding date is unknown. Shrine tradition says it was founded in the {{ill|lt=Yōrō|Q1373061|WD=Q1373061}} era ({{ill|lt=717|Q23532|WD=Q23532|lt=717}}–{{ill|lt=724|Q23549|WD=Q23549}}). Another tradition places it in the {{ill|lt=Tenchō|Q1373138|WD=Q1373138}} era ({{ill|lt=824|Q30353|WD=Q30353|lt=824}}–{{ill|lt=834|Q30381|WD=Q30381}}). It is an ancient shrine recorded in the {{ill|lt=Rikkokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168}} and the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}. * {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301}} ** Vol. 6: In the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Ninmyō|Q349271|WD=Q349271}}, 3rd month of {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 4 ({{ill|lt=837|Q25093|WD=Q25093}}), together with Sashino no Kami of Niihari District, it appears as "'''Ōkunitama no Kami of Makabe District'''" with the note "並預官社。以比年特有靈驗也" (it was admitted to the government shrine register due to especially notable divine efficacy in that year). ** Vol. 15: In the same era, year 12 ({{ill|lt=846|Q30454|WD=Q30454}}), "奉授常陸國无位'''大國玉神'''從五位下" (granted Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade). * {{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}} ** Vol. 5: In the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243}}, 9th month of {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 3 ({{ill|lt=861|Q23913|WD=Q23913}}), "授常陸國從五位下'''主玉神'''從五位上" (promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade). ** Among historical records, only the ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' records the deity name as "Shutama no Kami"; whether this is the same deity as Ōkunitama no Kami is unclear. Since there are three shrines in Ibaraki claiming to correspond to "Shutama no Kami" (Oishi Shrine in Hokota City, and Kamo-Ōkami-Miko-Shutama Shrine in Sakuragawa City), this is treated as a {{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}} (disputed corresponding shrine). * {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} ** In {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325}} 5 (927), listed as one minor shrine in Makabe District, Hitachi Province: "'''Ōkunitama Shrine'''" (one of {{ill|lt=Hitachi Province|Q1055353|WD=Q1055353}}'s 28 shrines). In the 11th month of {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1699|Q7710|WD=Q7710}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsukuni|Q1194944|WD=Q1194944}} dedicated banners of the four gods and halberd-banners of the sun and moon. In the 4th month of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}), it was ranked as a gōsha. In October {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1992|Q2060|WD=Q2060}}), shrine buildings were renovated. Old regional gazetteers introduce a scenic site called the "Seven Wells of Okunitama": Miyamae no I, Kugutsu I, Kōshinmae I, Kisaki no I, Fukusen Koshiki I, Fukusen Kome I, and Fukusen Sake I. File:大國玉神社 生鳥居１.jpg|Eastern ikitorii (living torii) File:大國玉神社 生鳥居２.jpg|Western ikitorii (living torii) === Enshrined deities === ; Main deity : {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} ; Associated deities : {{ill|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kamo Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto|Q11634943|WD=Q11634943|lt=Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto}} ''Shinpen Hitachi no Kuni-shi'' states: "Called Kashima Daimyōjin. According to shrine tradition, there were two enshrined deities: the east was called Nantai-gū, and the west Nyotai-gū, also called Monoimi-gū." In earlier times it was called Kashima Daimyōjin, and there was a paired two-shrine structure of a male-deity shrine and female-deity shrine. The integration of Nyotai-gū is described as "at the beginning of Meiji, the priestly office of the western shrine was dissolved and succeeded by the priest of the eastern shrine"Hitachi no Kuni-kei Sugiyama Shiki., indicating that this occurred in the Meiji period. The present shrine site is considered the continuation of Nantai-gū. The old site of Nyotai-gū is unknown, but Kisaki Shrine in the precincts enshrines Ōkuninushi's principal consort {{ill|lt=Suseribime|Q8191715|WD=Q8191715|lt=Suseribime-no-Mikoto}}. There is also another Kisaki Shrine east of Ōkunitama Shrine (an independent shrine: {{ill|lt=tanritsu|Q7882536|WD=Q7882536|lt=independent shrine}}), but its deity is "Kimi no Gozen," wife of {{ill|lt=Taira no Masakado|Q1207652|WD=Q1207652}}, and its {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shintai|1=UNKNOWN}} is a wooden female figure (Kisaki Shrine sacred image; designated cultural property of Sakuragawa City). Regarding the deity, one account says: "Perhaps Kashima Ōkami was enshrined; this is also evidenced by 23 songs transmitted at this shrine, all praising the divine power of Kashima deity." It further argues that "Ōkunitama-no-Kami" or "Kuni-gami" was an honorific title for deities who pacified each province, and in Hitachi Province it was another name for Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto. === Precinct shrines === * Kisaki Shrine ({{ill|lt=Suseribime|Q8191715|WD=Q8191715|lt=Suseribime-no-Mikoto}}) * Yasaka Shrine ({{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}) * Aoma Shrine ({{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami}}, {{ill|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Minakanushi|Q2842779|WD=Q2842779}}) * Shichijūgohashira Shrine (collective shrine of the myriad deities; Yaoyorozu no Kami) File:大國玉神社 摂社 青麻神社.jpg|Aoma Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 七拾五柱神社（八百萬神集合社）.jpg|Shichijūgohashira Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 后神社.jpg|Kisaki Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 八坂神社.jpg|Yasaka Shrine === Festivals === * {{ill|lt=January 3|Q2156|WD=Q2156}}: Kuwe no Matsuri (Kuwauchi Festival) – a {{ill|lt=ta-asobi|Q11577415|WD=Q11577415}} ritual praying for a good harvest; designated intangible folk cultural property of Sakuragawa City. * {{ill|lt=January 4|Q2168|WD=Q2168}}: Sayado Meguri Festival – formally called "Ōkunitama Shrine Mass Procession of the Seventy-Five Subsidiary Shrines"; participants carry a large sacred sakaki branch and beat drums while touring subsidiary shrines in the Okunitama district to pray for household safety; designated intangible folk cultural property of Sakuragawa City. * {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982}}: Annual festival === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} {{茨城県の神社一覧目次}} {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおくにたましんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Okunitama Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Okunitama Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:茨城県の神社]] [[Category:常陸国の式内社]] [[Category:茨城県の旧郷社]] [[Category:桜川市の建築物]] [[Category:桜川市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Okunitama Shrine |画像= [[File:大國玉神社 拝殿.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273}}{{ill|lt=Sakuragawa|Q990350|WD=Q990350}} Okunitama 1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor)
former {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system#various shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Gōsha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |別名 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982}} |神事 = Kuwe no Matsuri (Kuwauchi Festival)
Sayado Meguri Festival |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki|label=Okunitama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:大國玉神社 拝殿の扁額.jpg|thumb|right|245px|Plaque on the worship hall]] [[File:大國玉神社 本殿.jpg|thumb|right|245px|Main hall]] '''Okunitama Shrine''' (おおくにたまじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Sakuragawa|Q990350|WD=Q990350}}, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273}}, enshrining {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}. Its nickname is Myōjin-samaIbaraki Prefectural Shrine Association: [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/ibaraki/kensei/jinja/12178.html Shrine guide within the prefecture]. Retrieved November 16, 2013.. Its former name was Kashima Daimyōjin (Kashima Myōjin)''Shinpen Hitachi no Kuni-shi''.. It is listed as a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|lt=Hitachi Province|Q1055353|WD=Q1055353}}, Makabe District, minor shrine). Its former rank was {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system#various shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Gōsha}}. It is also written in old characters as '''大國玉神社'''. === History === The founding date is unknown. Shrine tradition says it was founded in the {{ill|lt=Yōrō|Q1373061|WD=Q1373061}} era ({{ill|lt=717|Q23532|WD=Q23532|lt=717}}–{{ill|lt=724|Q23549|WD=Q23549}}). Another tradition places it in the {{ill|lt=Tenchō|Q1373138|WD=Q1373138}} era ({{ill|lt=824|Q30353|WD=Q30353|lt=824}}–{{ill|lt=834|Q30381|WD=Q30381}}). It is an ancient shrine recorded in the {{ill|lt=Rikkokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168}} and the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}. * {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301}} ** Vol. 6: In the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Ninmyō|Q349271|WD=Q349271}}, 3rd month of {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 4 ({{ill|lt=837|Q25093|WD=Q25093}}), together with Sashino no Kami of Niihari District, it appears as "'''Ōkunitama no Kami of Makabe District'''" with the note "並預官社。以比年特有靈驗也" (it was admitted to the government shrine register due to especially notable divine efficacy in that year). ** Vol. 15: In the same era, year 12 ({{ill|lt=846|Q30454|WD=Q30454}}), "奉授常陸國无位'''大國玉神'''從五位下" (granted Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade). * {{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}} ** Vol. 5: In the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243}}, 9th month of {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 3 ({{ill|lt=861|Q23913|WD=Q23913}}), "授常陸國從五位下'''主玉神'''從五位上" (promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade). ** Among historical records, only the ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' records the deity name as "Shutama no Kami"; whether this is the same deity as Ōkunitama no Kami is unclear. Since there are three shrines in Ibaraki claiming to correspond to "Shutama no Kami" (Oishi Shrine in Hokota City, and Kamo-Ōkami-Miko-Shutama Shrine in Sakuragawa City), this is treated as a {{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}} (disputed corresponding shrine). * {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} ** In {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325}} 5 (927), listed as one minor shrine in Makabe District, Hitachi Province: "'''Ōkunitama Shrine'''" (one of {{ill|lt=Hitachi Province|Q1055353|WD=Q1055353}}'s 28 shrines). In the 11th month of {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1699|Q7710|WD=Q7710}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsukuni|Q1194944|WD=Q1194944}} dedicated banners of the four gods and halberd-banners of the sun and moon. In the 4th month of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}), it was ranked as a gōsha. In October {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1992|Q2060|WD=Q2060}}), shrine buildings were renovated. Old regional gazetteers introduce a scenic site called the "Seven Wells of Okunitama": Miyamae no I, Kugutsu I, Kōshinmae I, Kisaki no I, Fukusen Koshiki I, Fukusen Kome I, and Fukusen Sake I. File:大國玉神社 生鳥居１.jpg|Eastern ikitorii (living torii) File:大國玉神社 生鳥居２.jpg|Western ikitorii (living torii) === Enshrined deities === ; Main deity : {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} ; Associated deities : {{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kamo Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto|Q11634943|WD=Q11634943|lt=Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto}} ''Shinpen Hitachi no Kuni-shi'' states: "Called Kashima Daimyōjin. According to shrine tradition, there were two enshrined deities: the east was called Nantai-gū, and the west Nyotai-gū, also called Monoimi-gū." In earlier times it was called Kashima Daimyōjin, and there was a paired two-shrine structure of a male-deity shrine and female-deity shrine. The integration of Nyotai-gū is described as "at the beginning of Meiji, the priestly office of the western shrine was dissolved and succeeded by the priest of the eastern shrine"Hitachi no Kuni-kei Sugiyama Shiki., indicating that this occurred in the Meiji period. The present shrine site is considered the continuation of Nantai-gū. The old site of Nyotai-gū is unknown, but Kisaki Shrine in the precincts enshrines Ōkuninushi's principal consort {{ill|lt=Suseribime|Q8191715|WD=Q8191715|lt=Suseribime-no-Mikoto}}. There is also another Kisaki Shrine east of Ōkunitama Shrine (an independent shrine: {{ill|lt=tanritsu|Q7882536|WD=Q7882536|lt=independent shrine}}), but its deity is "Kimi no Gozen," wife of {{ill|lt=Taira no Masakado|Q1207652|WD=Q1207652}}, and its {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shintai|1=UNKNOWN}} is a wooden female figure (Kisaki Shrine sacred image; designated cultural property of Sakuragawa City). Regarding the deity, one account says: "Perhaps Kashima Ōkami was enshrined; this is also evidenced by 23 songs transmitted at this shrine, all praising the divine power of Kashima deity." It further argues that "Ōkunitama-no-Kami" or "Kuni-gami" was an honorific title for deities who pacified each province, and in Hitachi Province it was another name for Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto. === Precinct shrines === * Kisaki Shrine ({{ill|lt=Suseribime|Q8191715|WD=Q8191715|lt=Suseribime-no-Mikoto}}) * Yasaka Shrine ({{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}) * Aoma Shrine ({{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami}}, {{ill|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Minakanushi|Q2842779|WD=Q2842779}}) * Shichijūgohashira Shrine (collective shrine of the myriad deities; Yaoyorozu no Kami) File:大國玉神社 摂社 青麻神社.jpg|Aoma Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 七拾五柱神社（八百萬神集合社）.jpg|Shichijūgohashira Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 后神社.jpg|Kisaki Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 八坂神社.jpg|Yasaka Shrine === Festivals === * {{ill|lt=January 3|Q2156|WD=Q2156}}: Kuwe no Matsuri (Kuwauchi Festival) – a {{ill|lt=ta-asobi|Q11577415|WD=Q11577415}} ritual praying for a good harvest; designated intangible folk cultural property of Sakuragawa City. * {{ill|lt=January 4|Q2168|WD=Q2168}}: Sayado Meguri Festival – formally called "Ōkunitama Shrine Mass Procession of the Seventy-Five Subsidiary Shrines"; participants carry a large sacred sakaki branch and beat drums while touring subsidiary shrines in the Okunitama district to pray for household safety; designated intangible folk cultural property of Sakuragawa City. * {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982}}: Annual festival === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} {{茨城県の神社一覧目次}} {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおくにたましんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Okunitama Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Okunitama Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:茨城県の神社]] [[Category:常陸国の式内社]] [[Category:茨城県の旧郷社]] [[Category:桜川市の建築物]] [[Category:桜川市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Okunitama Shrine |画像= [[File:大國玉神社 拝殿.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273}}{{ill|lt=Sakuragawa|Q990350|WD=Q990350}} Okunitama 1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor)
former {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system#various shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Gōsha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |別名 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982}} |神事 = Kuwe no Matsuri (Kuwauchi Festival)
Sayado Meguri Festival |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki|label=Okunitama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:大國玉神社 拝殿の扁額.jpg|thumb|right|245px|Plaque on the worship hall]] [[File:大國玉神社 本殿.jpg|thumb|right|245px|Main hall]] '''Okunitama Shrine''' (おおくにたまじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Sakuragawa|Q990350|WD=Q990350}}, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273}}, enshrining {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}. Its nickname is Myōjin-samaIbaraki Prefectural Shrine Association: [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/ibaraki/kensei/jinja/12178.html Shrine guide within the prefecture]. Retrieved November 16, 2013.. Its former name was Kashima Daimyōjin (Kashima Myōjin)''Shinpen Hitachi no Kuni-shi''.. It is listed as a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|lt=Hitachi Province|Q1055353|WD=Q1055353}}, Makabe District, minor shrine). Its former rank was {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system#various shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Gōsha}}. It is also written in old characters as '''大國玉神社'''. === History === The founding date is unknown. Shrine tradition says it was founded in the {{ill|lt=Yōrō|Q1373061|WD=Q1373061}} era ({{ill|lt=717|Q23532|WD=Q23532|lt=717}}–{{ill|lt=724|Q23549|WD=Q23549}}). Another tradition places it in the {{ill|lt=Tenchō|Q1373138|WD=Q1373138}} era ({{ill|lt=824|Q30353|WD=Q30353|lt=824}}–{{ill|lt=834|Q30381|WD=Q30381}}). It is an ancient shrine recorded in the {{ill|lt=Rikkokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168}} and the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}. * {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301}} ** Vol. 6: In the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Ninmyō|Q349271|WD=Q349271}}, 3rd month of {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 4 ({{ill|lt=837|Q25093|WD=Q25093}}), together with Sashino no Kami of Niihari District, it appears as "'''Ōkunitama no Kami of Makabe District'''" with the note "並預官社。以比年特有靈驗也" (it was admitted to the government shrine register due to especially notable divine efficacy in that year). ** Vol. 15: In the same era, year 12 ({{ill|lt=846|Q30454|WD=Q30454}}), "奉授常陸國无位'''大國玉神'''從五位下" (granted Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade). * {{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}} ** Vol. 5: In the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243}}, 9th month of {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 3 ({{ill|lt=861|Q23913|WD=Q23913}}), "授常陸國從五位下'''主玉神'''從五位上" (promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade). ** Among historical records, only the ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' records the deity name as "Shutama no Kami"; whether this is the same deity as Ōkunitama no Kami is unclear. Since there are three shrines in Ibaraki claiming to correspond to "Shutama no Kami" (Oishi Shrine in Hokota City, and Kamo-Ōkami-Miko-Shutama Shrine in Sakuragawa City), this is treated as a {{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}} (disputed corresponding shrine). * {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} ** In {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325}} 5 (927), listed as one minor shrine in Makabe District, Hitachi Province: "'''Ōkunitama Shrine'''" (one of {{ill|lt=Hitachi Province|Q1055353|WD=Q1055353}}'s 28 shrines). In the 11th month of {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1699|Q7710|WD=Q7710}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsukuni|Q1194944|WD=Q1194944}} dedicated banners of the four gods and halberd-banners of the sun and moon. In the 4th month of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}), it was ranked as a gōsha. In October {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1992|Q2060|WD=Q2060}}), shrine buildings were renovated. Old regional gazetteers introduce a scenic site called the "Seven Wells of Okunitama": Miyamae no I, Kugutsu I, Kōshinmae I, Kisaki no I, Fukusen Koshiki I, Fukusen Kome I, and Fukusen Sake I. File:大國玉神社 生鳥居１.jpg|Eastern ikitorii (living torii) File:大國玉神社 生鳥居２.jpg|Western ikitorii (living torii) === Enshrined deities === ; Main deity : {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} ; Associated deities : {{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kamo Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto|Q11634943|WD=Q11634943|lt=Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto}} ''Shinpen Hitachi no Kuni-shi'' states: "Called Kashima Daimyōjin. According to shrine tradition, there were two enshrined deities: the east was called Nantai-gū, and the west Nyotai-gū, also called Monoimi-gū." In earlier times it was called Kashima Daimyōjin, and there was a paired two-shrine structure of a male-deity shrine and female-deity shrine. The integration of Nyotai-gū is described as "at the beginning of Meiji, the priestly office of the western shrine was dissolved and succeeded by the priest of the eastern shrine"Hitachi no Kuni-kei Sugiyama Shiki., indicating that this occurred in the Meiji period. The present shrine site is considered the continuation of Nantai-gū. The old site of Nyotai-gū is unknown, but Kisaki Shrine in the precincts enshrines Ōkuninushi's principal consort {{ill|lt=Suseribime|Q8191715|WD=Q8191715|lt=Suseribime-no-Mikoto}}. There is also another Kisaki Shrine east of Ōkunitama Shrine (an independent shrine: {{ill|lt=tanritsu|Q7882536|WD=Q7882536|lt=independent shrine}}), but its deity is "Kimi no Gozen," wife of {{ill|lt=Taira no Masakado|Q1207652|WD=Q1207652}}, and its {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shintai|1=UNKNOWN}} is a wooden female figure (Kisaki Shrine sacred image; designated cultural property of Sakuragawa City). Regarding the deity, one account says: "Perhaps Kashima Ōkami was enshrined; this is also evidenced by 23 songs transmitted at this shrine, all praising the divine power of Kashima deity." It further argues that "Ōkunitama-no-Kami" or "Kuni-gami" was an honorific title for deities who pacified each province, and in Hitachi Province it was another name for Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto. === Precinct shrines === * Kisaki Shrine ({{ill|lt=Suseribime|Q8191715|WD=Q8191715|lt=Suseribime-no-Mikoto}}) * Yasaka Shrine ({{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}) * Aoma Shrine ({{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami}}, {{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Minakanushi|Q2842779|WD=Q2842779}}) * Shichijūgohashira Shrine (collective shrine of the myriad deities; Yaoyorozu no Kami) File:大國玉神社 摂社 青麻神社.jpg|Aoma Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 七拾五柱神社（八百萬神集合社）.jpg|Shichijūgohashira Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 后神社.jpg|Kisaki Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 八坂神社.jpg|Yasaka Shrine === Festivals === * {{ill|lt=January 3|Q2156|WD=Q2156}}: Kuwe no Matsuri (Kuwauchi Festival) – a {{ill|lt=ta-asobi|Q11577415|WD=Q11577415}} ritual praying for a good harvest; designated intangible folk cultural property of Sakuragawa City. * {{ill|lt=January 4|Q2168|WD=Q2168}}: Sayado Meguri Festival – formally called "Ōkunitama Shrine Mass Procession of the Seventy-Five Subsidiary Shrines"; participants carry a large sacred sakaki branch and beat drums while touring subsidiary shrines in the Okunitama district to pray for household safety; designated intangible folk cultural property of Sakuragawa City. * {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982}}: Annual festival === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} {{茨城県の神社一覧目次}} {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおくにたましんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Okunitama Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Okunitama Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:茨城県の神社]] [[Category:常陸国の式内社]] [[Category:茨城県の旧郷社]] [[Category:桜川市の建築物]] [[Category:桜川市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

(Sakuragawa) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '茨城県桜川市大国玉1', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Okunitama-jinja (Sakuragawa)}} [[JA:大国玉神社 (桜川市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11433665}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Okunitama Shrine |画像= [[File:大國玉神社 拝殿.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273}}{{ill|lt=Sakuragawa|Q990350|WD=Q990350}} Okunitama 1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor)
former {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system#various shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Gōsha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |別名 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982}} |神事 = Kuwe no Matsuri (Kuwauchi Festival)
Sayado Meguri Festival |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki|label=Okunitama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:大國玉神社 拝殿の扁額.jpg|thumb|right|245px|Plaque on the worship hall]] [[File:大國玉神社 本殿.jpg|thumb|right|245px|Main hall]] '''Okunitama Shrine''' (おおくにたまじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Sakuragawa|Q990350|WD=Q990350}}, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273}}, enshrining {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}. Its nickname is Myōjin-samaIbaraki Prefectural Shrine Association: [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/ibaraki/kensei/jinja/12178.html Shrine guide within the prefecture]. Retrieved November 16, 2013.. Its former name was Kashima Daimyōjin (Kashima Myōjin)''Shinpen Hitachi no Kuni-shi''.. It is listed as a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|lt=Hitachi Province|Q1055353|WD=Q1055353}}, Makabe District, minor shrine). Its former rank was {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system#various shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Gōsha}}. It is also written in old characters as '''大國玉神社'''. === History === The founding date is unknown. Shrine tradition says it was founded in the {{ill|lt=Yōrō|Q1373061|WD=Q1373061}} era ({{ill|lt=717|Q23532|WD=Q23532|lt=717}}–{{ill|lt=724|Q23549|WD=Q23549}}). Another tradition places it in the {{ill|lt=Tenchō|Q1373138|WD=Q1373138}} era ({{ill|lt=824|Q30353|WD=Q30353|lt=824}}–{{ill|lt=834|Q30381|WD=Q30381}}). It is an ancient shrine recorded in the {{ill|lt=Rikkokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168}} and the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}. * {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301}} ** Vol. 6: In the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Ninmyō|Q349271|WD=Q349271}}, 3rd month of {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 4 ({{ill|lt=837|Q25093|WD=Q25093}}), together with Sashino no Kami of Niihari District, it appears as "'''Ōkunitama no Kami of Makabe District'''" with the note "並預官社。以比年特有靈驗也" (it was admitted to the government shrine register due to especially notable divine efficacy in that year). ** Vol. 15: In the same era, year 12 ({{ill|lt=846|Q30454|WD=Q30454}}), "奉授常陸國无位'''大國玉神'''從五位下" (granted Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade). * {{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}} ** Vol. 5: In the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243}}, 9th month of {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 3 ({{ill|lt=861|Q23913|WD=Q23913}}), "授常陸國從五位下'''主玉神'''從五位上" (promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade). ** Among historical records, only the ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' records the deity name as "Shutama no Kami"; whether this is the same deity as Ōkunitama no Kami is unclear. Since there are three shrines in Ibaraki claiming to correspond to "Shutama no Kami" (Oishi Shrine in Hokota City, and Kamo-Ōkami-Miko-Shutama Shrine in Sakuragawa City), this is treated as a {{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}} (disputed corresponding shrine). * {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} ** In {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325}} 5 (927), listed as one minor shrine in Makabe District, Hitachi Province: "'''Ōkunitama Shrine'''" (one of {{ill|lt=Hitachi Province|Q1055353|WD=Q1055353}}'s 28 shrines). In the 11th month of {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1699|Q7710|WD=Q7710}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsukuni|Q1194944|WD=Q1194944}} dedicated banners of the four gods and halberd-banners of the sun and moon. In the 4th month of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}), it was ranked as a gōsha. In October {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1992|Q2060|WD=Q2060}}), shrine buildings were renovated. Old regional gazetteers introduce a scenic site called the "Seven Wells of Okunitama": Miyamae no I, Kugutsu I, Kōshinmae I, Kisaki no I, Fukusen Koshiki I, Fukusen Kome I, and Fukusen Sake I. File:大國玉神社 生鳥居１.jpg|Eastern ikitorii (living torii) File:大國玉神社 生鳥居２.jpg|Western ikitorii (living torii) === Enshrined deities === ; Main deity : {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} ; Associated deities : {{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kamo Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto|Q11634943|WD=Q11634943|lt=Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto}} ''Shinpen Hitachi no Kuni-shi'' states: "Called Kashima Daimyōjin. According to shrine tradition, there were two enshrined deities: the east was called Nantai-gū, and the west Nyotai-gū, also called Monoimi-gū." In earlier times it was called Kashima Daimyōjin, and there was a paired two-shrine structure of a male-deity shrine and female-deity shrine. The integration of Nyotai-gū is described as "at the beginning of Meiji, the priestly office of the western shrine was dissolved and succeeded by the priest of the eastern shrine"Hitachi no Kuni-kei Sugiyama Shiki., indicating that this occurred in the Meiji period. The present shrine site is considered the continuation of Nantai-gū. The old site of Nyotai-gū is unknown, but Kisaki Shrine in the precincts enshrines Ōkuninushi's principal consort {{ill|lt=Suseribime|Q8191715|WD=Q8191715|lt=Suseribime-no-Mikoto}}. There is also another Kisaki Shrine east of Ōkunitama Shrine (an independent shrine: {{ill|lt=tanritsu|Q7882536|WD=Q7882536|lt=independent shrine}}), but its deity is "Kimi no Gozen," wife of {{ill|lt=Taira no Masakado|Q1207652|WD=Q1207652}}, and its {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shintai|1=UNKNOWN}} is a wooden female figure (Kisaki Shrine sacred image; designated cultural property of Sakuragawa City). Regarding the deity, one account says: "Perhaps Kashima Ōkami was enshrined; this is also evidenced by 23 songs transmitted at this shrine, all praising the divine power of Kashima deity." It further argues that "Ōkunitama-no-Kami" or "Kuni-gami" was an honorific title for deities who pacified each province, and in Hitachi Province it was another name for Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto. === Precinct shrines === * Kisaki Shrine ({{ill|lt=Suseribime|Q8191715|WD=Q8191715|lt=Suseribime-no-Mikoto}}) * Yasaka Shrine ({{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}) * Aoma Shrine ({{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami}}, {{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Minakanushi|Q2842779|WD=Q2842779}}) * Shichijūgohashira Shrine (collective shrine of the myriad deities; Yaoyorozu no Kami) File:大國玉神社 摂社 青麻神社.jpg|Aoma Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 七拾五柱神社（八百萬神集合社）.jpg|Shichijūgohashira Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 后神社.jpg|Kisaki Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 八坂神社.jpg|Yasaka Shrine === Festivals === * {{ill|lt=January 3|Q2156|WD=Q2156}}: Kuwe no Matsuri (Kuwauchi Festival) – a {{ill|lt=ta-asobi|Q11577415|WD=Q11577415}} ritual praying for a good harvest; designated intangible folk cultural property of Sakuragawa City. * {{ill|lt=January 4|Q2168|WD=Q2168}}: Sayado Meguri Festival – formally called "Ōkunitama Shrine Mass Procession of the Seventy-Five Subsidiary Shrines"; participants carry a large sacred sakaki branch and beat drums while touring subsidiary shrines in the Okunitama district to pray for household safety; designated intangible folk cultural property of Sakuragawa City. * {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982}}: Annual festival === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} {{茨城県の神社一覧目次}} {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおくにたましんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Okunitama Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Okunitama Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:茨城県の神社]] [[Category:常陸国の式内社]] [[Category:茨城県の旧郷社]] [[Category:桜川市の建築物]] [[Category:桜川市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

(Sakuragawa) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '茨城県桜川市大国玉1', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Okunitama-jinja (Sakuragawa)}} [[JA:大国玉神社 (桜川市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11433665}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Okunitama Shrine |画像= [[File:大國玉神社 拝殿.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Sakuragawa|Q990350|WD=Q990350|1=Sakuragawa, Ibaraki}} Okunitama 1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}} |社格 = {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} (minor)
former {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system#various shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Gōsha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |別名 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982|1=November 15}} |神事 = Kuwe no Matsuri (Kuwauchi Festival)
Sayado Meguri Festival |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki|label=Okunitama Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:大國玉神社 拝殿の扁額.jpg|thumb|right|245px|Plaque on the worship hall]] [[File:大國玉神社 本殿.jpg|thumb|right|245px|Main hall]] '''Okunitama Shrine''' (おおくにたまじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Sakuragawa|Q990350|WD=Q990350|1=Sakuragawa, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}, enshrining {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}. Its nickname is Myōjin-samaIbaraki Prefectural Shrine Association: [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/ibaraki/kensei/jinja/12178.html Shrine guide within the prefecture]. Retrieved November 16, 2013.. Its former name was Kashima Daimyōjin (Kashima Myōjin)''Shinpen Hitachi no Kuni-shi''.. It is listed as a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} ({{ill|lt=Hitachi Province|Q1055353|WD=Q1055353|1=Hitachi Province}}, Makabe District, minor shrine). Its former rank was {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system#various shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Gōsha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. It is also written in old characters as '''大國玉神社'''. === History === The founding date is unknown. Shrine tradition says it was founded in the {{ill|lt=Yōrō|Q1373061|WD=Q1373061|1=Yōrō}} era ({{ill|lt=717|Q23532|WD=Q23532|lt=717|1=717}}–{{ill|lt=724|Q23549|WD=Q23549|1=724}}). Another tradition places it in the {{ill|lt=Tenchō|Q1373138|WD=Q1373138|1=Tenchō}} era ({{ill|lt=824|Q30353|WD=Q30353|lt=824|1=824}}–{{ill|lt=834|Q30381|WD=Q30381|1=834}}). It is an ancient shrine recorded in the {{ill|lt=Rikkokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|1=Rikkokushi}} and the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. * {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}} ** Vol. 6: In the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Ninmyō|Q349271|WD=Q349271|1=Emperor Ninmyō}}, 3rd month of {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}} 4 ({{ill|lt=837|Q25093|WD=Q25093|1=837}}), together with Sashino no Kami of Niihari District, it appears as "'''Ōkunitama no Kami of Makabe District'''" with the note "並預官社。以比年特有靈驗也" (it was admitted to the government shrine register due to especially notable divine efficacy in that year). ** Vol. 15: In the same era, year 12 ({{ill|lt=846|Q30454|WD=Q30454|1=846}}), "奉授常陸國无位'''大國玉神'''從五位下" (granted Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade). * {{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}} ** Vol. 5: In the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243|1=Emperor Seiwa}}, 9th month of {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 3 ({{ill|lt=861|Q23913|WD=Q23913|1=861}}), "授常陸國從五位下'''主玉神'''從五位上" (promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade). ** Among historical records, only the ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' records the deity name as "Shutama no Kami"; whether this is the same deity as Ōkunitama no Kami is unclear. Since there are three shrines in Ibaraki claiming to correspond to "Shutama no Kami" (Oishi Shrine in Hokota City, and Kamo-Ōkami-Miko-Shutama Shrine in Sakuragawa City), this is treated as a {{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} (disputed corresponding shrine). * {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} ** In {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} 5 (927), listed as one minor shrine in Makabe District, Hitachi Province: "'''Ōkunitama Shrine'''" (one of {{ill|lt=Hitachi Province|Q1055353|WD=Q1055353|1=Hitachi Province}}'s 28 shrines). In the 11th month of {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1699|Q7710|WD=Q7710|1=1699}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsukuni|Q1194944|WD=Q1194944|1=Tokugawa Mitsukuni}} dedicated banners of the four gods and halberd-banners of the sun and moon. In the 4th month of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}), it was ranked as a gōsha. In October {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1992|Q2060|WD=Q2060|1=1992}}), shrine buildings were renovated. Old regional gazetteers introduce a scenic site called the "Seven Wells of Okunitama": Miyamae no I, Kugutsu I, Kōshinmae I, Kisaki no I, Fukusen Koshiki I, Fukusen Kome I, and Fukusen Sake I. File:大國玉神社 生鳥居１.jpg|Eastern ikitorii (living torii) File:大國玉神社 生鳥居２.jpg|Western ikitorii (living torii) === Enshrined deities === ; Main deity : {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}} ; Associated deities : {{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}}, {{ill|lt=Kamo Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto|Q11634943|WD=Q11634943|lt=Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Kamo-wake-ikazuchi-no-Kami}} ''Shinpen Hitachi no Kuni-shi'' states: "Called Kashima Daimyōjin. According to shrine tradition, there were two enshrined deities: the east was called Nantai-gū, and the west Nyotai-gū, also called Monoimi-gū." In earlier times it was called Kashima Daimyōjin, and there was a paired two-shrine structure of a male-deity shrine and female-deity shrine. The integration of Nyotai-gū is described as "at the beginning of Meiji, the priestly office of the western shrine was dissolved and succeeded by the priest of the eastern shrine"Hitachi no Kuni-kei Sugiyama Shiki., indicating that this occurred in the Meiji period. The present shrine site is considered the continuation of Nantai-gū. The old site of Nyotai-gū is unknown, but Kisaki Shrine in the precincts enshrines Ōkuninushi's principal consort {{ill|lt=Suseribime|Q8191715|WD=Q8191715|lt=Suseribime-no-Mikoto|1=Suseri-Hime}}. There is also another Kisaki Shrine east of Ōkunitama Shrine (an independent shrine: {{ill|lt=tanritsu|Q7882536|WD=Q7882536|lt=independent shrine|1=Non-denominational}}), but its deity is "Kimi no Gozen," wife of {{ill|lt=Taira no Masakado|Q1207652|WD=Q1207652|1=Taira no Masakado}}, and its {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shintai|1=UNKNOWN}} is a wooden female figure (Kisaki Shrine sacred image; designated cultural property of Sakuragawa City). Regarding the deity, one account says: "Perhaps Kashima Ōkami was enshrined; this is also evidenced by 23 songs transmitted at this shrine, all praising the divine power of Kashima deity." It further argues that "Ōkunitama-no-Kami" or "Kuni-gami" was an honorific title for deities who pacified each province, and in Hitachi Province it was another name for Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto. === Precinct shrines === * Kisaki Shrine ({{ill|lt=Suseribime|Q8191715|WD=Q8191715|lt=Suseribime-no-Mikoto|1=Suseri-Hime}}) * Yasaka Shrine ({{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}) * Aoma Shrine ({{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}, {{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Kami|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Minakanushi|Q2842779|WD=Q2842779|1=Ame-no-Minakanushi}}) * Shichijūgohashira Shrine (collective shrine of the myriad deities; Yaoyorozu no Kami) File:大國玉神社 摂社 青麻神社.jpg|Aoma Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 七拾五柱神社（八百萬神集合社）.jpg|Shichijūgohashira Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 后神社.jpg|Kisaki Shrine File:大國玉神社 摂社 八坂神社.jpg|Yasaka Shrine === Festivals === * {{ill|lt=January 3|Q2156|WD=Q2156|1=January 3}}: Kuwe no Matsuri (Kuwauchi Festival) – a {{ill|lt=ta-asobi|Q11577415|WD=Q11577415|1=Ta-asobi}} ritual praying for a good harvest; designated intangible folk cultural property of Sakuragawa City. * {{ill|lt=January 4|Q2168|WD=Q2168|1=January 4}}: Sayado Meguri Festival – formally called "Ōkunitama Shrine Mass Procession of the Seventy-Five Subsidiary Shrines"; participants carry a large sacred sakaki branch and beat drums while touring subsidiary shrines in the Okunitama district to pray for household safety; designated intangible folk cultural property of Sakuragawa City. * {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982|1=November 15}}: Annual festival === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} {{茨城県の神社一覧目次}} {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおくにたましんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Okunitama Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Okunitama Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:茨城県の神社]] [[Category:常陸国の式内社]] [[Category:茨城県の旧郷社]] [[Category:桜川市の建築物]] [[Category:桜川市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]
